questions.question: Given `int arr[10]; int *p = arr;`, what does `p + 1` advance the address by?
questions.explanation: Pointer arithmetic is *typed*: `p + 1` advances by `sizeof(*p)` bytes. For `int *`, that's typically 4. This is exactly why `arr[i]` (which is `*(arr + i)`) works for any element type — the size scaling is baked in.

questions.question: Why does `arr[i]` produce the same value as `i[arr]` in C?
options: By definition, `a[b]` is `*(a + b)`. Addition is commutative, so `*(arr + i)` = `*(i + arr)` = `i[arr]`.
It's a typo that the compiler ignores
questions.explanation: Subscript is syntactic sugar for pointer addition. Since `+` is commutative, `arr[i]` and `i[arr]` denote the same address dereference. Don't use `i[arr]` in real code (readers will flinch) — but knowing why it works tells you what subscript really means.

questions.question: Casting `(uint32_t *)&someFloat` and dereferencing is:
options: Undefined behavior per the strict-aliasing rule (except via char* or explicit unions). Use memcpy to convert bit representations portably.
questions.explanation: The C standard requires you to access an object only through its declared type's pointer (with a few exceptions: `char *`, signed/unsigned variants, struct-as-first-member). Violations are UB; modern compilers optimize aggressively under that assumption and produce surprising code. The portable bit-cast is `memcpy(&dst, &src, sizeof src);` which optimizers turn into a register move.

questions.question: After `int *p = malloc(40); free(p);`, what is `*p` (reading)?
options: Undefined behavior — the storage is no longer your program's; the allocator may have reused it. ASan flags this as use-after-free.
The last value written before free
questions.explanation: Use-after-free is UB. The allocator may have already reused the bytes for another allocation; reads return whatever's there, writes corrupt unrelated state. Best practice: `SAFE_FREE(p)` macro that sets p = NULL after free. Rust's borrow checker prevents this at compile time.

questions.question: Function pointers' type carries the function's:
options: Signature (return type + argument types) — so the compiler knows how to compile a call through the pointer
questions.explanation: `int (*fn)(int, int)` declares a pointer to a function taking two ints and returning int. The signature drives ABI placement (which args in which registers) and validates that the assignment `fn = some_func` is type-compatible. Calling through a mismatched function pointer is UB.

questions.question: Why is `void *` used as the universal pointer in `malloc`?
options: `void *` carries an address but no type, so the caller can cast it to any object-pointer type they need. C requires no explicit cast (unlike C++).
questions.explanation: `malloc` doesn't know what type you're allocating, so it returns `void *`. The caller declares the type at use: `int *p = malloc(n * sizeof(int));`. C performs the implicit cast; C++ requires explicit. You cannot dereference or do arithmetic on `void *` until you cast — it's a 'type-eraser.'
